county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Some early territorial records are thin due to sparse settlement.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1856 from Iron County. Beaver was an early Mormon settlement along the wagon route between Salt Lake City and southern Utah. Fort Cameron (1872-1883) was established here during tensions over federal anti-polygamy enforcement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Beaver County Recorder in Beaver City holds deed records from 1856. LDS ward records on FamilySearch are the primary genealogical source for early families — the Beaver Ward was organized in 1856. Fort Cameron military records at NARA document soldiers stationed here during anti-polygamy campaigns (1870s-80s). The Beaver County courthouse (1882) is one of Utah's oldest surviving public buildings. Mining districts at San Francisco Mountains and Frisco produced silver mining records in the 1870s-80s. Check Iron County for pre-1856 records. DUP (Daughters of Utah Pioneers) museum in Beaver has pioneer family files.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books, deeds of trust, and mortgages from 1856 to present. County Recorder maintains index books.

Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints ward and branch records for Beaver County communities. Includes blessing records (births), baptisms, confirmations, ordinations, marriages, membership records, and death notices. Functions as de facto vital records for most of Utah's history.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1856. State registration from 1887 (territorial), comprehensive from 1905.

Historical newspapers from Beaver County communities. Utah Digital Newspapers (J. Willard Marriott Library, U of U) has extensive digitized Utah papers. Chronicling America has additional titles.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records well-preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1856. Box Elder County was settled by Mormon colonists sent by Brigham Young to establish communities in northern Utah. Brigham City was a cooperative community experiment under Lorenzo Snow. The transcontinental railroad's Golden Spike ceremony occurred at Promontory Summit in 1869.
county_metadata.research_tips: Box Elder County Recorder in Brigham City has deed records from 1856. LDS ward records are essential — Brigham City Ward organized 1851, one of the earliest in northern Utah. The Golden Spike National Historical Park preserves transcontinental railroad records; Central Pacific and Union Pacific employee rosters identify railroad workers. Lorenzo Snow's cooperative movement (1864-1885) generated business records documenting community members. The Bear River Massacre site (1863) is in Box Elder County — military records at NARA document the engagement. Corinne, Utah was a non-Mormon railroad town; its records differ from typical LDS communities. Box Elder County DUP Museum has family histories. FamilySearch has extensive microfilmed ward records for this area.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. County records intact from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1856. Cache Valley was settled by Mormon pioneers from 1856. Utah State University (originally Utah Agricultural College) founded in Logan in 1888. Cache County has been the agricultural heartland of northern Utah.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cache County Recorder in Logan holds deed records from 1859. LDS ward records cover Logan and surrounding communities from the late 1850s and are the primary genealogical source. Utah State University's Special Collections and Archives (Merrill-Cazier Library) hold extensive Cache Valley family papers, pioneer journals, and oral histories. The Cache Valley Historical Society maintains local records. Logan Temple records (1884+) are available through FamilySearch. Scandinavian converts to Mormonism settled in Cache Valley in large numbers — check Danish, Swedish, and Norwegian parish records cross-referenced with LDS emigration records. Hyrum, Wellsville, Mendon, and Smithfield each have distinct ward histories. Cache County had significant dairy industry records.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major fire losses. Some early mining company records dispersed or lost when companies closed.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1894 from Emery County. Named for extensive coal deposits. Carbon County's history is dominated by coal mining — the county became Utah's most ethnically diverse area as immigrants from Greece, Italy, Japan, Finland, Austria-Hungary, and other nations came to work the mines.
county_metadata.research_tips: Carbon County Recorder in Price has deed records from 1894. Carbon County is Utah's most ethnically diverse county — Greek Orthodox church records (Assumption Greek Orthodox Church, Price, est. 1916), Japanese Buddhist temple records, and Italian Catholic parish records are all critical sources alongside LDS ward records. The Helper Mining Museum and Western Mining & Railroad Museum document the coal mining heritage. Castle Gate mine disaster (1924, 172 killed) records at the Utah State Archives identify miners and families. The College of Eastern Utah (now USU Eastern) has oral histories of immigrant mining families. Spring Glen, Sunnyside, Kenilworth, and Castle Gate each had distinct ethnic communities. Greek coffee house records and mutual aid society records document the Greek community. Check Emery County for pre-1894 records. Carbon County newspapers (Sun Advocate, Price) document mining families extensively.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records sparse due to very small population (historically under 1,000 residents).
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1917 from Uintah County. Daggett is Utah's least populated county. Named for Ellsworth Daggett, surveyor. The Flaming Gorge area was historically used for ranching and later flooded by the reservoir (1964).
county_metadata.research_tips: Daggett County Recorder in Manila has deed records from 1917. With a population often under 1,000, Daggett County's records are thin but well-preserved. Ranching and outlaw history dominate — Butch Cassidy's Brown's Park hideout was in the county. Bureau of Reclamation records for Flaming Gorge Dam (1964) at NARA Denver document displaced families. The John Jarvie Historic Ranch preserves early settlement records. Ashley National Forest records at NARA may document timber and grazing permits identifying early residents. Check Uintah County for pre-1917 records. FamilySearch has limited but useful microfilmed records. The small population makes newspaper research in the Manila and Dutch John papers highly productive for tracing families.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records well-preserved from territorial organization.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1850 as one of the original six counties of Utah Territory. Davis County lies between Salt Lake City and Ogden. Early Mormon settlement from 1847-1848. Hill Air Force Base established 1940.
county_metadata.research_tips: Davis County Recorder in Farmington holds deed records from 1850, among the earliest in the state. LDS ward records for Farmington, Bountiful, Kaysville, Centerville, and Layton wards are the foundation of genealogical research here — organized from 1847-1850. Davis County Clipper (newspaper, 1891+) has extensive local coverage. Hill Air Force Base (1940+) civilian employment records may be accessible through OPM for later families. Bountiful was Brigham Young's secondary residence; church leadership records document prominent families. The Utah State Railroad Museum at Ogden Union Station has records relevant to Davis County railroad families. Davis County was the home of many early Mormon polygamous families — Edmunds Act prosecutions (1882+) generated court records at the National Archives. DUP museums in Farmington and Bountiful have pioneer family collections.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Settlement began late (1905 Uintah Reservation opening).
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1914 from Wasatch County. Named for the Duchesne River. The county was part of the Uintah Reservation until 1905 when the reservation was opened to white settlement. Significant Ute presence continues.
county_metadata.research_tips: Duchesne County Recorder in Duchesne has deed records from 1914. The county's late opening (1905 Uintah Reservation allotment) means settlement records are relatively recent. Bureau of Indian Affairs allotment records at NARA Denver document Ute tribal members who received individual allotments. Homestead records at BLM GLO website identify settlers who filed after the 1905 opening. Starvation Reservoir (1970) displaced some ranching families — Bureau of Reclamation records at NARA. LDS ward records for Duchesne, Roosevelt, Myton, and Tabiona communities on FamilySearch. Check Wasatch County for any pre-1914 territorial records. The Uintah County Library in Vernal has regional history materials that include Duchesne area families. Duchesne County had a small but notable population of non-Mormon ranchers and homesteaders.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Some early settlement records thin due to remote location.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1880 from Sanpete and Sevier counties. Named for George W. Emery, territorial governor. The Castle Country area was settled by Mormon colonists in the late 1870s. Coal mining developed in the eastern part of the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Emery County Recorder in Castle Dale holds deed records from 1880. LDS ward records for Castle Dale, Huntington, Ferron, Green River, and Orangeville are the primary genealogical source for early families. The Museum of the San Rafael in Castle Dale has pioneer artifacts and family files. Emery County was a parent to Carbon County (1894) — check records in both counties for mining-era families. The D&RGW Railroad brought coal mining to the area; railroad employment records are at the Denver & Rio Grande Western collection at the Colorado Railroad Museum and the Utah State Archives. Cleveland-Lloyd Dinosaur Quarry area has homestead records at BLM. Remote communities like Green River had distinct histories tied to river crossings and later to uranium mining (1950s). WPA oral histories at the Utah State Archives cover Emery County pioneers.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books, deeds of trust, and mortgages from 1880 to present. County Recorder maintains index books.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records sparse in some periods due to small population.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1882 from Iron County. Named for President James A. Garfield. Garfield County encompasses Bryce Canyon and parts of Capitol Reef and Grand Staircase-Escalante. Remote Mormon settlements were established in the 1870s-1880s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Garfield County Recorder in Panguitch has deed records from 1882. LDS ward records for Panguitch, Escalante, Tropic, Boulder, and Henrieville are the primary sources — Escalante Ward (organized 1875) documents one of the most isolated communities in the contiguous United States. The Panguitch DUP Museum has pioneer journals and photographs. Boulder, Utah was the last community in the contiguous US to receive mail by mule train (until 1940). National Park Service records for Bryce Canyon (established 1928) and Capitol Reef at NARA document early settlers within park boundaries who were displaced. Check Iron County for pre-1882 records. Garfield County's Black Hawk War (1865-1872) skirmishes are documented in territorial militia records at the Utah State Archives. Cattle ranching records are significant for the Escalante and Boulder areas.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Early records thin due to sparse settlement before the uranium boom.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1890 from Emery County. Moab area was settled in the 1870s-1880s by Mormon ranchers and farmers. Grand County experienced booms in uranium (1950s) and tourism (Arches National Park).
county_metadata.research_tips: Grand County Recorder in Moab holds deed records from 1890. Early records focus on ranching and farming families — the Moab area had fewer than 500 residents until the uranium boom of the 1950s. LDS ward records for the Moab Ward (organized 1882) cover early settlers. The Dan O'Laurie Museum and Museum of Moab have local history collections. Uranium mining claims (1950s) at BLM and Atomic Energy Commission records at NARA document the boom-era population. Arches National Park records at NARA include information about homesteaders within park boundaries. Check Emery County for pre-1890 records. The Moab Times-Independent (1896+) is an excellent source for local families. French-Canadian fur traders and early explorers documented in early records. Potash mining records (1960s+) at the Utah State Archives.
